flashcards.front: Differentiate between Simplex, Half-Duplex, and Full-Duplex transmission modes with real-world examples.
flashcards.back: Simplex: unidirectional communication (keyboard→CPU). Half-Duplex: bidirectional but alternating (walkie-talkie, Ethernet hubs). Full-Duplex: simultaneous bidirectional (telephone, full-duplex Ethernet switches). The choice impacts bandwidth utilization and network efficiency.

flashcards.front: Explain the function of a Network Hub and its limitations in modern networks.
flashcards.back: A hub is a Layer 1 (Physical) device that broadcasts incoming data to all ports regardless of the destination MAC address. It creates a shared collision domain, causing performance degradation as nodes increase. Modern networks have largely replaced hubs with switches due to these limitations.

flashcards.front: How does a Network Switch differ from a Hub? Why is switching preferred in LANs?
flashcards.back: A switch operates at Layer 2 (Data Link) and learns MAC addresses, forwarding frames only to the specific destination port (CAM table). This reduces collisions, increases security (traffic isolation), and improves bandwidth by creating separate collision domains per port. Switches are preferred for their superior performance and scalability.

flashcards.front: What is the primary function of a Router in a network? How does it differ from a Switch?
flashcards.back: A router operates at Layer 3 (Network) and forwards packets between different networks based on IP addresses and routing tables (e.g., determining best path). Unlike switches that forward frames within the same network using MAC addresses, routers connect heterogeneous networks (LAN to WAN) and provide logical addressing and inter-network communication.

flashcards.front: Explain the role of a Repeater in network communication. How does it differ from an Amplifier?
flashcards.back: A repeater is a Layer 1 device that receives, regenerates (not amplifies), and retransmits weakened signals to extend network segment length (e.g., Ethernet 10Base2/10Base5). Unlike amplifiers that boost both signal and noise, repeaters reconstruct clean digital signals, maintaining signal integrity.

flashcards.front: What is the function of a Gateway in computer networking?
flashcards.back: A gateway operates at all OSI layers and connects two dissimilar networks using different protocols (e.g., LAN to WAN, or TCP/IP to IBM SNA). It performs protocol conversion, addressing, and routing functions. Unlike routers that connect similar networks, gateways translate between fundamentally different network architectures.

flashcards.front: Describe the functions of a Modem in network communication. Differentiate between Analog, DSL, and Cable modems.
flashcards.back: A modem (Modulator-Demodulator) converts digital data to analog signals for transmission over analog lines (WAN) and back to digital. Analog modems (dial-up) use telephone lines; DSL uses existing telephone lines with higher bandwidth; cable modems use cable TV infrastructure. All provide internet connectivity by interfacing LAN protocols with WAN transmission media.

flashcards.front: A company has 100 employees needing dedicated bandwidth. Which network device should they use and why? Justify with technical reasoning.
flashcards.back: They should use a Managed Switch with VLAN capability. An unmanaged hub would create a single collision domain causing severe performance degradation. A router would be overkill for internal LAN communication. A switch provides dedicated bandwidth per port (full-duplex), reduces collisions, and managed switches allow traffic segmentation via VLANs, ensuring Quality of Service (QoS) for critical applications.
